results.title: Block Matrix Formulation of Linear Attention Mechanisms | Franz Louis Cesista
results.content:
Now, parallel associative scan might seem the best choice, and indeed it already suffices for some architectures like Mamba 1. However, it requires a lot more (shared) memory and communication between the processing units (and therefore materialization to the HBM). And it also doesnвҖҷt fully utilize the tensor cores. But with chunk-wise parallelism, we only need to store the current state in the shared memory, and use matrix multiplications to compute the next chunk-level state. This way, we donвҖҷt have to materialize the $S\_N$s to the HBM at all, and we can fully utilize the tensor cores. Hence why most flash linear attention kernels use chunk-wise parallelism. [...] ## Chunk-wise Parallelism [Section Flagged for Review]#

Since the update operations of linear attention mechanisms we discussed above are associativeвҖ“i.e., the order in which we вҖңcombineвҖқ the updates doesnвҖҷt matterвҖ“we can perform the computations in multiple ways:

1. The Fully Recurrent Form where we update the state as we loop through the tokens/update matrices one by one,
2. The Fully-Parallel Associative Scan Form where we hierarchically combine the updates in a tree-like structure, and
3. The Chunk-wise Parallel Form (Hua et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2023) which is a compromise between the two where we divide the sequence into chunks first, combine intra-chunk updates in parallel, and then combine the chunk-level updates in a recurrent manner. [...] > \At inference time, we need to process the input tokens first before generating outputs. This is called the вҖңpre-fillingвҖқ stage. And chunk-wise parallelism works better here. After that, we can then use the recurrent form to generate the outputs.

A better way to think of chunk-wise parallelism is as multi-step online gradient descent, but instead of updating the state $n\_h$ times per token, we update the state $n\_c$ times per chunk where $n\_c = N/C$ is the number of tokens per chunk and $C$ is the number of chunks. Thus, we just reuse our results from the previous section!

To make the connection more explicit, letвҖҷs reindex Equation $(1)$ as $[\cdot]\_i = [\cdot]\_{\lceil i/n\_c \rceil,\space (i-1) \% n\_c + 1}$:

Thus a P2P communication operation Recv should be performed to pull KVtвҲ’1 from rank (i вҲ’1) to rank i. results.title: [PDF] Gated Linear Attention Transformers with Hardware-Efficient Training
results.content:
Parallel form.
The parallel form could be as efficient as FLASHATTENTION using similar I/O optimization techniques, as demonstrated by Qin et al. (2023b). However, the high number of FLOPs (due to the quadratic complexity) makes the long-sequence training expensive, the same issue that the naВЁ Дұve implementation of softmax attention would suffer from.
Chunkwise form. [...] Chunkwise form.
The chunkwise parallel form, which interpolates between the parallel and recurrent forms with an extra вҖңparameterвҖқ C, makes it possible to more easily make the above tradeoffs for fine-grained optimization. Unlike the recurrent form, most operations can be done via matmuls, enabling the use of tensor cores (if C is set to a multiple of 16). Though the chunkwise training algorithm has been discussed before in the literature (Hua et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2023a), most implementations are not I/O-aware and thus slower than FLASHATTENTION for moderate sequence lengths (e.g., 2K-4K). [...] 4.2 Chunkwise Parallel Form of GLA We derive a chunkwise form of GLA similar to the chunkwise form of basic linear attention (В§2.2). Here the intra-chunk operation implements the above parallel form 6This form resembles extrapolatable position encoding (Sun et al., 2023b) in that the term inside the exponential can be viewed as a data-dependent relative position factor.

results.title: Linear Attention Fundamentals | Hailey Schoelkopf
results.content:
to obtain the right answer to avoid вҖңseeing into the futureвҖқ. This pointwise multiplication by our causal mask \(M \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times L}\) prevents us from using associativity to compute \(\phi(K)^TV\) firstвҖ“losing the better complexity of linear attention we claimed. We need to compute \(\phi(Q)\phi(K)^T\) , requiring \(O(L^2)\) time.

### Chunkwise Form

Luckily, not all is lost. Hua et al. (2022)propose a solution they term the вҖңchunkwise parallel formвҖқ for linear attention training, which is later extended by othersfor even better efficiency. [...] This chunkwise formulation allows us to interpolate between the parallel and recurrent forms, choosing \(C\) based on which is fastest, and ends up being faster than full recurrence because we can take advantage of fast matrix multiplications without paying a cost quadratic in \(L\) !

This chunkwise formulation is also adopted by the Mamba-2 / SSDarchitectureвҖ“chunkwise parallelism is very hardware-friendly. This chunked algorithm is sometimes called вҖңFlash Linear AttentionвҖқ for this reason .

## Conclusion

In this post, weвҖҷve seen:

 How вҖңlinear attentionвҖқ is derived and originally motivated
 How this can be viewed as an RNN with a matrix-valued state
 How to make training these theoretically-linear-complexity models efficient on hardware [...] However, when we want to actually train these models, we run into problems: computing \(S\_l = S\_{l-1} + k\_l^Tv\_l\) then \(o\_l = q\_l S\_l\) via looping over our entire sequence length ( \(l \in [1, L]\) ) (full вҖңrecurrent modeвҖқ) is generally prohibitively slow, despite costing \(O(Ldd')\) FLOP. We are forced to compute each of our \(L\) timesteps sequentially (instead of in parallel as in softmax attention), and must save our potentially-large state \(S\_l\) to memory and read it back from memory at each timestep.Approaches like the custom kernel for performing a parallel scan in Mamba-1 can mitigate this by keeping the state in higher-bandwidth SRAM, but this imposes limitations on how large we can make our state without being forced to incur IO costs.
